english: v. K'a-rkyáṅ (kha rkyang).
v. K'a-rtsáṅ (kha rtsang).
or ཁ་སར་པ་ཎ་ K'a-sar-pa-ṇa (kha sar pa Na) n. of a deity Glr.; Tar. p. 110 gives a (rather absurd) legend concerning the origin of the name.

1. (cf. sgal (sgal)) burden, load, k'al k'yér-ba (khal khyer ba) to carry a burden; k'al-gyi stéṅ-du (khal gyi steng du) on the top of the luggage Glr.; k'al 'gél-ba (khal 'gel ba) to load a burden, to put a load upon, k'al bógs-pa (khal bogs pa) to take off the burden, to unload; load, freight; as a fixed quantity, lúg-k'al (lug khal) a sheep-load, bóṅ-k'al (bong khal) load of an ass; brui k'al (bru'i khal) a load of corn. — 2. bushel, a dry measure = 20 bre (bre); therefore = a score or 20 things of the same kind; in W. *k'al-gcig* (khal gcig) frq. for nyí-śu (nyi shu), also with respect to persons; gśór-k'al (gshor khal) a 'measuring-score', 20 bre (bre), actually measured, as is usual with corn; 'dégs-k'al ('degs khal) a 'weighing-score', the weight of 20 points on the steel-yard (rgya-ma (rgya ma)), in weighing wood, hay, butter etc.
n. of a Mongol tribe, Khal-ka.
stunned, insensible Thgy.
the best sort of wool for manufacturing shawls, coming from Jang-thang.
1. wether, castrated ram. — 2. sow-thistle, Sonchus.

beast of burden, sumpter-mule B., C. k'al-ma-rnams bzáṅ-la skyél-ba (khal ma rnams bzang la skyel ba) to drive beasts of burden to the pasture, to turn them on grass-land Glr.; Schr.; *mi k'al nyis-kyi la* (mi khal nyis kyi la) C. payment for carriers and beasts of burden; though in W. it might be understood as: payment for twice twenty men.
also k'a-ri (kha ri), k'a-rú (kha ru) twenty bushels.

instr. of k'a (kha); k'as-lén-pa (khas len pa) etc. v. k'a (kha), 4; k'as-skóṅ (khas skong) = k'á-skóṅ (kha skong), q.v.; k'as-stóṅ (khas stong) with an empty stomach; k'as-dmáṅ (khas dmang), k'as-žán (khas zhan), weak, poor.
numerical figure: 32.
C.: *k'yíu* (khyi'u) a cutting-out knife.
1. numerical figure: 62. — 2. for k'u-lu (khu lu) (?) Lil.
Cs. '1. uncle. 2. an address'(?)
a hut, cottage, constructed of branches Lh.

p. n. Kunawar, also Bissáhar, country on the upper Sutledj, bordering on Tibet, and inhabited in the northern part by Tibetans. Here are situated Kanám, a monastery with a considerable collection of Tibetan books, and Poo, a missionary station of the Church of the United Brethren, founded 1865.

1. fluid, liquid, also (but less frq.) k'u-čú (khu chu); lhuṅ-bzéd bkrús-pai k'u-ba (lhung bzed bkrus pa'i khu ba), the fluid in which a beggar's bowl has been washed Tar.; krús-k'u (khrus khu) dish-wash, swill Tar.; brás-k'u (bras khu) Cs.: rice-soup, Schr.: rice-water; śiṅ-k'u (shing khu), rtsá-k'u (rtsa khu) the sap of trees, of plants Cs.; śá-k'u (sha khu) broth, gravy; már-k'u (mar khu) melted butter. — 2. semen virile, k'u-ba byín-pa (khu ba byin pa) emittere semen; k'u-k'rág (khu khrag) the mixture of the semen with the uterine blood, by which process, acc. to Indian physiology, the fetus is formed, Med.

uncle, on the father's side B. and C.; p'a-k'u (pha khu) father and uncle; k'u-dbón (khu dbon) and k'u-tsán (khu tshan) uncle and nephew. But owing to polyandry, the degrees of kindred lose their precision, in as far as all the brothers that have become the husbands of one wife may be called 'father' by the children.

B., also k'u-gyúg (khu gyug) and yug (yug). cuckoo, called byá-yi ryál-po (bya yi rgyal po) and described as a sweetly singing bird, whence prob. Cs. has conjectured nightingale, which however is scarcely known in Tibet. — k'u-byug-rtsá (khu byug rtsa) n. of a medicinal herb.
Lh. purse, money-bag, col. for kug-ma (khug ma).
Cs. the clinched hand, fist, k'u-tsúr snún-pa (khu tshur snun pa) (Sch. also rgyáb-pa (rgyab pa)) to strike with the fist. This signifi-
